properties.name: Kalibangan (Indus-Sarasvati site)

facts: India's third-largest Indus-Sarasvati site — on the Ghaggar palaeochannel; excavated by B.B. Lal (ASI, 1961-69).
Only Indus site with pre-Harappan and Harappan settlement stacked one over the other — plus the world's earliest known ploughed field (Pre-Harappan, ~2900 BCE).
The seven "fire altars" arranged in a row in the citadel are unique to Kalibangan — the strongest evidence of Vedic-adjacent ritual at any Indus site.
Its collapse c. 1750 BCE is attributed to the drying of the Sarasvati/Ghaggar — a template case for climate-driven civilisational collapse.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Ahar (Aharan Sanskriti — Chalcolithic)

facts: Type-site of Rajasthan's Chalcolithic "Ahar (Aharan) Culture" — a copper-using pre-iron farming civilisation of the Berach basin.
Excavated by H.D. Sankalia (Deccan College, 1954-56) — famous "Black-and-Red Ware" (BRW) ceramic sequence.
Cotton spinning wheels & spindles here (with Kalibangan) prove cotton textile use in Rajasthan going back ~4000 years.
Site sits on Udaipur's northern edge, on the Ayad river — the "Aravalli lakeside civilisation" cognate of Malwa's Kayatha and Navdatoli.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Balathal (Chalcolithic – Early Historic)
properties.period: Chalcolithic (~3000-1500 BCE) + Early Historic (~500 BCE-200 CE)

facts: Chalcolithic Ahar-Banas culture site 42 km north-east of Udaipur; excavated by Deccan College with Rajasthan Vidyapeeth (1993-2000).
Uncovered a huge fortified enclosure with copper-using farming settlements — one of India's earliest fortified villages.
Also yielded India's oldest known "sanyasi burial" — a c. 500 BCE seated skeleton in a ritual posture.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Gilund (Ahar-Banas Chalcolithic)

facts: Twin site of Ahar-Banas Chalcolithic culture (with Balathal & Ahar) on the Banas river — jointly excavated by Deccan College + University of Pennsylvania (1959-60; 2003-05).
Yielded a hoard of 100+ clay seal-impressions — the most ceramic sealings from any Chalcolithic Indian site.
Two mud-brick platforms suggest an early public architecture tradition in the Aravalli piedmont.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Bagor (Mesolithic)

facts: Rajasthan's type Mesolithic site on the Kothari river; excavated by V.N. Misra (Deccan College, 1967-72).
One of the largest microlithic tool assemblages in India — thousands of geometric microliths and animal-bone remains.
Domestication of cattle here (~5000 BCE) is among the earliest evidence of Indian pastoral economy.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Bairath (Viratnagar) — Ashokan Buddhist

facts: The Matsya Mahajanapada capital of Virata Nagar mentioned in the Mahabharata — where the Pandavas spent their year of ajnatavas.
Two Ashokan edicts survive here — the Bhabru Minor Rock Edict (Ashoka's letter to the Sangha) plus a fragmentary standing pillar edict.
Bairath's circular Buddhist stupa was the westernmost brick Buddhist monument in ancient India — the state's earliest known Buddhist site.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Noh (Yakshi & Jakhababa Sculptures)

facts: The Yakshi statue from Noh (life-size, red sandstone) is one of the earliest surviving Shunga-period female figures.
The Jakhababa (Yaksha) sculpture — a huge male Yaksha attendant figure — represents the Shunga sculptural tradition in Bharatpur.
Both statues are housed in the Government Museum, Bharatpur (Lohagarh Fort).

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Nagari (Madhyamika — Sivi Republic Capital)
properties.period: Sivi Janapada + Post-Maurya + Gupta (5th c. BCE-6th c. CE)

facts: Ancient Madhyamika — capital of the Sivi (Shibi) republic (janapada) north of Chittorgarh, on the Bedach river.
Yielded some of India's earliest coins bearing the "Majhamikaya Sibi Janapadasa" legend — 3rd-2nd c. BCE.
Colonel James Tod first identified Madhyamika with the Nagari ruins (1820s) — the site anchors the earliest urban history of Mewar.
Grand temples of the Gupta era and a Vaishnava enclosure survive as ruins on the site.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Rairh (Post-Maurya Coinage Centre)
properties.period: Post-Maurya to early Gupta (3rd c. BCE-4th c. CE)

facts: Second-largest coin-hoard site in ancient India (after Kaushambi) — over 3,000 punch-marked and Yaudheya coins excavated in 1938-39.
The huge terracotta-mould workshop suggests Rairh was a regional mint town serving the Malava-Yaudheya coin economy.
Rairh + Nagar (Tonk district) together dominate Rajasthan's numismatic corpus for the immediate post-Maurya centuries.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Rangmahal (Kushana-Gupta era)

facts: On the dried Sarasvati/Ghaggar channel; excavated by Rikke Hanna-Rooijakkers, Swedish mission (1952-54).
Terracotta plaques of Kushana-Gupta iconography (Krishna Leela, Ramayan scenes) — the state's finest early historical terracotta corpus.
Along with Kalibangan, evidence of continuous habitation on the Sarasvati/Ghaggar palaeochannel from Harappan through Gupta eras.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Ganeshwar (Chalcolithic Copper Hoard)

facts: Chalcolithic copper-hoard site in the Khetri copper belt — the "supplier" for Harappan copper artefacts, per J.P. Joshi's hypothesis.
Yielded India's largest single Chalcolithic copper-object cache — 400+ pieces (arrows, harpoons, rings, celts).
Site anchors the Chalcolithic copper economy of the Aravalli's northern edge — the ancestor of modern Khetri's HCL smelter.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Bundi Cave Paintings (Prehistoric Rock Art)

facts: Rock-shelter paintings at Garadada, Gopalpura, Chatarkund and Rambali in the Bundi hills — Rajasthan's prime prehistoric-rock-art corpus.
Depictions include hunting scenes, cattle, elephants and dancing figures; discovered by Om Prakash Sharma in 1997.
Compared with Bhimbetka, Bundi's pigment survival is remarkable given the Vindhyan limestone substrate.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins

properties.name: Mangarh Hill (Modern Memorial to Bhil Resistance)

facts: Site of the 1913 Mangarh Massacre — 1,500 Bhil tribals killed by British forces during Govind Guru's Samp Sabha meeting.
Notified as a National Monument (2022) — the National Mangarh Dham Memorial was dedicated by the PM in November 2022.
A rare "modern archaeological site" — its stratigraphy is being formally documented by the ASI since 2023.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 11 Ch 2 + Class 12 Chs 6, 8 (Cultural Rajasthan) + ASI Rajasthan bulletins
